lens.included_activities: Federal, state, and local government establishments administering, promoting, and developing general economic resources, including economic development, small-business support, tourism promotion, trade commissions, consumer protection, and general economic statistics; under the fixed acquisition lens there are no qualifying independent private LMM firms.
lens.excluded_activities: Private economic-development consultants, chambers of commerce, nonprofit grantees, Small Business Development Center operators, job placement and vocational rehabilitation providers, industry-specific transportation, utility, agricultural, and commercial-sector regulators classified elsewhere, shells, captive internal units, non-control financing vehicles, and all entities outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Public agencies administer programs, grants, research, statistics, outreach, technical assistance, promotion, and consumer-protection functions for businesses, communities, policymakers, and residents, funded through appropriations and intergovernmental program funds rather than market-priced recurring outsourced contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: none; applying the fixed default lens produces no qualifying private-firm population because NAICS 926110 comprises government economic-program establishments

a.bridge: BLS describes economist and compliance-officer work across broader public and private populations, while GAO reports AI use across selected federal agencies rather than a wage-weighted task census for NAICS 926110. I map survey and data collection, statistical analysis, forecasting, policy and program research, application intake, eligibility and completeness checks, grant monitoring, compliance review, document drafting, reporting, status tracking, public inquiries, outreach content, and knowledge search to exposure. I discount stakeholder negotiation, policy judgment, field verification, enforcement discretion, relationship building, public hearings, award decisions, and accountable approval. The proxy may overstate exposure in small local offices with generalist and face-to-face roles and understate it in large statistical agencies.
a.rationale: Economic-program administration is dominated by information, documents, analysis, communication, and repeat program workflows, making more than half of wage-weighted work plausibly exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Human authority and local stakeholder work keep exposure well below full automation.
a.change_evidence: A wage- and time-weighted workforce study across economic-development, tourism, trade, consumer-protection, small-business, and statistical agencies, separating already-automated tasks from new AI capabilities, would materially change the range.
caveats: No source provides a six-digit occupational or task distribution for NAICS 926110.
Federal AI inventories may not represent smaller state and local agencies with older systems and more direct public contact.

rho.rationale: Writing, internal search, summarization, coding assistance, data cleaning, forecasting support, application triage, grant monitoring, public-information tools, and program-status tracking can be implemented within five years. Realized substitution is constrained by privacy, cybersecurity, records requirements, data quality, bias and hallucination, benefits and grant due process, procurement, legacy systems, fragmented federal-state-local governance, budget and technical capacity, public transparency, workforce rules, and required human approvals.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployment cohorts at economic-development and statistical agencies reporting task adoption, staff hours, error and appeal rates, processing time, privacy incidents, integration cost, procurement duration, user access, and human-review burden would materially narrow the range.
caveats: GAO found rapid growth in reported federal AI use cases but did not measure labor savings or implementation in this six-digit industry.
Low-risk mission support can move faster than eligibility, award, enforcement, or policy decisions that materially affect the public.

e.rationale: The official NAICS definition explicitly comprises government establishments administering, promoting, and developing economic resources. Such agencies do not satisfy the fixed population of independent LMM firms supplying outsourced services to external customers, so eligibility is structurally zero even though frozen n is missing and must not be replaced.
e.change_evidence: Only a classification change or authoritative establishment-level evidence of privately owned firms properly classified in NAICS 926110, rather than consultants, nonprofit partners, or grantees classified elsewhere, would change the structural zero.
caveats: Frozen n is missing; the zero is an eligibility share based on public ownership, not an estimate of government establishment count.
Private vendors and nonprofit delivery partners may be acquisition candidates in other industries but are outside this record.

s5.rationale: With no eligible independent firms, no qualifying private control transfer can occur. Elections, appointments, agency reorganizations, program transfers, grant awards, contract awards, and privatization of selected functions do not transfer control of an eligible NAICS 926110 operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Authoritative evidence that a privately controlled economic-program operator is properly classified in NAICS 926110 and can undergo a conventional control transaction would change the estimate.
caveats: This is a structural transaction definition, not a forecast of political leadership or civil-service turnover.
A contractor change or new grantee is a procurement or funding event rather than control transfer of the agency.

q.rationale: No defensible commercial-retention quantity exists because these agencies lack market-priced recurring contracts and a receipts denominator. Efficiency gains may become additional program capacity, faster processing, expanded analysis, better targeting, lower administrative spending, or reduced future appropriations, and those outcomes do not map consistently to retained gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: A public-value accounting framework with audited agency benefit realization and jurisdiction-specific appropriation rules showing how administrative savings are retained, reallocated, or returned would be needed to define and estimate an analogue.
caveats: Grant leverage, jobs supported, or constituent benefits are program outcomes rather than operator gross benefit.
Assigning a neutral retention value would falsely imply a commercial revenue mechanism.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the broad government sector and for occupations used in economic programs, while EDA reports current grants; none forecasts five-year constant-quality demand for the full NAICS 926110 service basket. I combine nearly flat projected 2024-34 government employment, 1% economist growth, 3% compliance-officer growth, and active 2025 EDA grant administration to anchor broadly stable demand. Employment can understate demand when productivity rises and overstate it when staffing reflects political budgets rather than service need; one federal agency's grants may not represent state, local, tourism, trade, consumer-protection, and statistics programs.
d5.rationale: Economic statistics, small-business support, consumer protection, tourism and trade promotion, and regional development remain recurring public functions. Global complexity, disasters, technology shifts, industrial policy, and local competition sustain demand, but appropriations, policy reversals, agency consolidation, grant-cycle expiration, and digital self-service can reduce the current basket, supporting a near-flat center and wide range.
d5.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-weighted five-year forecast of agency appropriations, programs, grant and assistance volumes, statistical products, consumer cases, tourism and trade activity, staffing, and legislated sunsets would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Broad occupation and sector projections include substantial activity outside NAICS 926110.
EDA's 312 grants and $423.8 million awarded in 2025 show current activity, not a five-year national demand trajectory.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five economic-program demand still requires an accountable public operator to exercise statutory authority, appropriate or award funds, certify official statistics, set program priorities, protect data, oversee vendors and grantees, and answer to elected officials and the public. Portals, self-service, consultants, nonprofits, and software can deliver information or analysis and remove parts of the service basket, but cannot independently own public authority or accountability.
o.change_evidence: Enacted privatization, automated statutory approvals without accountable agency review, or evidence that private platforms fully replace material economic-development, statistics, consumer-protection, or grant-administration volumes would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate concerns accountable operator necessity, not public employee counts or the degree of contractor use.
Some counseling, outreach, research, and technical assistance already can be delivered by partners classified outside this industry.

narrative.executive_view: General economic-program administration has substantial assistive AI potential because much of the work involves data, analysis, documents, grants, compliance, reporting, and public communication. Federal use cases are expanding, but governance and legacy constraints limit five-year realization. The code is structurally outside the acquisition lens: it comprises government establishments, frozen n is missing, eligibility and transfer shares are zero, and commercial retention cannot be defined from appropriations.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support economic data collection and cleaning, forecasts, policy research, application triage, eligibility and completeness checks, grant monitoring, compliance review, knowledge search, document drafting, translation, public inquiries, outreach content, and program-status reporting. Human officials remain necessary for policy choices, stakeholder negotiation, field verification, award and enforcement decisions, approvals, and accountability. GAO's federal evidence shows rapid adoption alongside policy, budget, technical, privacy, and governance obstacles.
narrative.value_capture: These agencies do not have a conventional customer-price and gross-margin mechanism. Productivity may create faster decisions, more analyzed data, avoided hiring, improved targeting, expanded service, or lower administrative cost, but appropriators may reallocate or remove the savings. Program benefits and private investment leveraged are public outcomes, not operator-retained commercial benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The official definition is explicitly limited to government establishments, including economic-development agencies, trade commissions, consumer-protection offices, small-business development agencies, and general economic statistical agencies. There is no eligible private LMM firm population. Private consultants, nonprofit partners, counselors, technology vendors, and grant recipients belong in other industries, and procurement changes are not control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand should remain broadly stable because governments continue to produce economic statistics, administer development and assistance programs, protect consumers, and promote local business, tourism, and trade. EDA reported 312 grants and $423.8 million awarded in 2025, while BLS projects nearly flat overall government employment and modest growth for economists and compliance officers. Funding cycles, political priorities, consolidations, and program sunsets create material downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive weaknesses are structural ineligibility and missing commercial retention. Operational risks include budget volatility, political change, procurement delays, fragmented jurisdictions, legacy systems, poor data, privacy and cybersecurity, biased or fabricated outputs, public-records obligations, accessibility, benefits and grant due process, workforce rules, vendor dependence, and difficulty measuring public value. Broad federal and occupational proxies may not generalize to local agencies.
narrative.principal_driver: Information-intensive economic analysis, statistics, grant, compliance, and communication workflows create meaningful AI-assisted capacity while core public functions persist.
narrative.principal_weakness: NAICS 926110 is government administration with no eligible private-firm population or conventional commercial-retention construct.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 926110 Administration of General Economic Programs
sources.supports: Official government-only scope covering administration, promotion, and development of economic resources, including economic-development, small-business, trade, consumer-protection, tourism, and statistical agencies.

sources.title: Economists
sources.supports: Task and demand proxy: economists research issues, collect and analyze data, forecast trends, advise government, and publish results; 52% work in federal, state, or local government and employment is projected to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Compliance Officers
sources.supports: Task and demand proxy: compliance work includes interpreting rules, risk assessment, audits, investigations, training, documentation, and program measurement; government employs 37% of the occupation and total employment is projected to grow 3% from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies
sources.supports: Implementation proxy: across 11 selected agencies, reported AI use cases rose from 571 in 2023 to 1,110 in 2024 and generative-AI cases from 32 to 282; agencies cited policy, budget, technical-resource, privacy, and governance challenges.

sources.title: Employment Projections, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Broad demand proxy projecting wage-and-salary government employment, excluding state and local education and hospitals, to decline 0.1% from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: U.S. Economic Development Administration Impact Overview
sources.supports: Current program-activity proxy: EDA reports 312 grants and $423.8 million awarded in 2025, with performance measures tied to annual budgets and appropriations.
